content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660           322-5901            322-6441            322-6444

October 9, 1981

Richard A. Smith, Esq.
Harland and Gromala
Attorneys at Law
P.O. Box 5
Fortuna, CA 95540

A-81-10-003

Dear Mr. Smith:

I am writing in response to your request for advice
from this office. As your letter indicated, your inquiry
results from the proposed hiring, by the Eureka School District,
of a certificated employee who is the wife of a member of
the Eureka Board of Education. The specific questions presented were:

(1) If the Board member, Mr. Ed DelBiaggio, discloses the spousal relationship, and then neither votes nor participates in the discussion leading up to the
hiring, can the remaining Board members, which constitute
a significant number to act, vote to hire the spouse as
a certificated employee?

(2) Does the requirement of Education Code Section
35237 cause the provisions of Education Code, Sections
35230, et seq., addressing conflict of interest situations
involving school board members, to supersede applicable
portions of the Political Reform Act?

In response to your first question, Government Code
Section 87100, 2/ prohibits a public official of a local
government agency from making, participating in making, or

1/ "The question of the validity or invalidity of a
contract or other transaction entered into by the governing
board ... is interested in such contract or transaction ...
shall be exclusively governed by the provisions of the Education
Code which ... shall supersede (1) any and all provisions of
law contained in any code or law of this state...."

2/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

Richard A. Smith, Esq.
October 9, 1981
Page Two

in any way attempting to use his official position to in-
fluence a "governmental decision in which he shows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest."2/ A "financial
interest" exists "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on: (c) Any source
of income.... (Section 87103(c).)

Under the Act, "income" includes an individual's community
property interest in his spouse's income (Section 82030(a)).
At first glance it would appear then that nonparticipation
by the Board of Education member, in decisions involving his
spouse, is required. Section 82030(b)(2), however, alters
the outcome. This section provides an exception which states,
"(b) Income ... does not include ... (2) Salary ... received
from a ... local ... government agency...." Since a school
district is a local governmental agency, any wages to the
employee, Mrs. DelBiaggio, are exempt from the definition of
income and the Board member is without a financial interest,
within the meaning of the Act.

While Mr. DelBiaggio may choose to abstain from parti-
cipating in the decision to hire his wife, the Political
Reform Act does not require it. We urge you to consider,
though, possible common law restrictions or statutory limita-
tions existing outside of the Political Reform Act, an example
of which is Section 1090.

This leads to your second question, which is whether
the conflict of interest provisions, provided for by the
Education Code, supersede the Political Reform Act. Section
81013 provides that the Legislature, or any other state or
local agency, may impose additional requirements on any
person if the requirements do not prevent the person from
complying with the Act. Where any act of the Legislature
conflicts with the Act, the Act prevails. The Education
Code sections you cited appear to set a higher standard of
conduct. Since they do not conflict with the Act, they may
be applied.

If you have any further questions, please feel free to
call me at 916/322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Janis L. Shank
Counsel
Legal Division

JLS:km

3/
A Board of Education member is within the scope of
this prohibition. (See Section 82041.)

Harland & Gromala
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
954 MAIN STREET
P. O. BOX 485
FORTUNA, CALIFORNIA 95540
(707) 725-4420

GERALD H. HARLAND
JOHN A. GROMALA
RICHARD A. SMITH
JAMES W. FRITZ
THOMAS BECKER
PATRICIA BURGESS

OF COUNSEL
COLLIS P. MAHAN

OTHER OFFICES
EUREKA
FERNDALE

September 24, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

ATTN: Legal Division

RE: Informal Opinion Letter under Government Code
Section 83114(b)

Ladies and Gentlemen:

I am an attorney representing Eureka City Schools, a
public entity of the State of California. In this capacity
I wish to obtain for one of the members of the Board of Education an informal Opinion Letter. The facts that necessitate the
opinion concern the school District hiring as a certificated
employee the spouse of a member of the Board of Education.
The specific questions are:

1. Under the Political Reform Act, if the Board
member neither votes nor takes part in the discussion that
leads up to the hiring and prior to discussion announces the
conflict position that he is in, can the remaining Board members who constitute a quorum, and constitute a sufficient
number to take action, vote to hire the spouse as a certificated
employee; and

2. As to transactions concerning conflict of interests of School Board members which are found at Sections
35230 and following of the Education Code, are they to be
followed in the place of the Political Reform Act because of
the requirements of Education Code Section 35237?

Your immediate attention to both of these matters would
be greatly appreciated.

Yours very truly,

RICHARD A. SMITH

RAS/lt

cc: Mr. M. McManus

As your letter indicated, your inquiry results from the proposed hiring, by the Eureka School District, of a certificated employee who is the wife of a member of the Eureka Board of Education. The specific questions presented were: (1) If the Board member, Mr. Ed DelBiaggio, discloses the spousal relationship, and then neither votes nor participates in the discussion leading up to the hiring, can the remaining Board members, which constitute a significant number to act, vote to hire the spouse as a certificated employee? (2) Does the requirement of Education Code Section 35237 cause the provisions of Education Code, Sections 35230, et seq., addressing conflict of interest situations involving school board members, to supersede applicable portions of the Political Reform Act? In response to your first question, Government Code Section 87100 prohibits a public official of a local government agency from making, participating in making, or 1/ "The question of the validity or invalidity of a contract or other transaction entered into by the governing board ... is interested in such contract or transaction ... shall be exclusively governed by the provisions of the Education Code which ... shall supersede (1) any and all provisions of law contained in any code or law of this state...." 2/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated. in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a "governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."3/ A "financial interest" exists "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (c) Any source of income.... (Section 87103(c).) Under the Act, "income" includes an individual's community property interest in his spouse's income (Section 82030(a)). At first glance it would appear then that nonparticipation by the Board of Education member, in decisions involving his spouse, is required. Section 82030(b)(2), however, alters the outcome. This section provides an exception which states, "(b) Income ... does not include ... (2) Salary ... received from a ... local ... government agency...." Since a school district is a local governmental agency, any wages to the employee, Mrs. DelBiaggio, are exempt from the definition of income and the Board member is without a financial interest, within the meaning of the Act. While Mr. DelBiaggio may choose to abstain from participating in the decision to hire his wife, the Political Reform Act does not require it. We urge you to consider, though, possible common law restrictions or statutory limitations existing outside of the Political Reform Act, an example of which is Section 1090. This leads to your second question, which is whether the conflict of interest provisions, provided for by the Education Code, supersede the Political Reform Act.
